metadata.summary: Google Shopping campaign setup, product feed management, Merchant Center authentication, product partition trees, bidding progression, and API gotchas for ecommerce catalog advertising.

content.title: Google Shopping Ads Campaign Management
content.summary: Master Google Shopping campaigns via API: feed setup, Merchant Center integration, product partition strategies, campaign structure, bidding progression, and authentication across two APIs.

why.p: Shopping campaigns differ fundamentally from Search: there is no ad copy, no keywords. Google assembles the ad from your product feed. Feed quality (titles, images, GTINs, categories) drives 80% of performance; bids drive the remaining 20%.

Shopping requires managing two separate APIs with different authentication scopes (Content API for Merchant Center, Google Ads API for campaigns). Product partition trees must be MECE to prevent silent gaps where SKUs receive no bid. Agents working with Shopping must understand feed optimization, Merchant Center suspension rules, currency differences between APIs, and bidding progression.

list.item: Setting up or scaling Google Shopping campaigns for ecommerce catalogs.
Building agents that manage product feeds, partition trees, or bid adjustments.
Automating feed quality audits, disapproval diagnosis, or feed upload workflows.
Integrating Shopping performance data into multi-channel campaign dashboards.
Small catalogs (under 50 SKUs) where manual Google Merchant Center management is simpler.
Non-ecommerce businesses or services without a product feed.
Performance Max campaigns — use google-pmax instead.

rule.statement: Validate the Merchant Center feed and verify zero critical disapprovals before creating Shopping campaigns; campaigns referencing a broken feed generate zero impressions.
rule.rationale: Shopping campaigns pull ads from the product feed. A feed with disapprovals means those products cannot serve. Creating campaigns before fixing disapprovals wastes setup time and delays launch.

list.item: Google Merchant Center account created and linked to Google Ads
Product feed uploaded with required attributes: id, title, description, link, image_link, price, availability, brand, gtin
Feed diagnostics show zero critical disapprovals on at least 95% of SKUs

rule.statement: Content API for Shopping (Merchant Center) uses separate authentication from the Google Ads API; agents must manage two sets of service-account credentials.
rule.rationale: Merchant Center operations (feed upload, disapproval audit) require the content scope, not the ads scope. Agents using the same credentials for both will get authentication errors on one of the APIs.

rule.statement: Set shopping_setting.merchant_id at campaign creation; it cannot be changed after the campaign is created.
rule.rationale: The Merchant Center ID is an immutable field once set. Agents that create campaigns without it must delete and recreate, losing any auction history.

list.item: advertising_channel_type: SHOPPING
shopping_setting.merchant_id: Merchant Center account ID (required at creation)
shopping_setting.sales_country: country code (e.g., "US")
shopping_setting.campaign_priority: 0 (Low), 1 (Medium), 2 (High)
Bidding: manual_cpc.enhanced_cpc_enabled=True initially
Status: PAUSED

rule.statement: Product partition trees must be MECE (mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive); every SKU must belong to exactly one partition node, always ending with an "everything else" catch-all subdivision.
rule.rationale: Missing a catch-all means SKUs not matching any explicit partition have no bid and cannot enter the auction. The campaign will run but silently miss a portion of the catalog.

list.item: Dimension types: BRAND, CATEGORY, CONDITION, CHANNEL, CHANNEL_EXCLUSIVITY
Each partition must have a value node and an "everything else" sibling node
Use campaign priority (0/1/2) to control which campaign wins when multiple campaigns target the same SKU

section.name: product-performance-reporting
section.p: Shopping reporting uses shopping_performance_view with product-level segments instead of keyword-level data.

list.item: segments.product_item_id — SKU identifier
segments.product_title — product title as uploaded
segments.product_brand — brand attribute
segments.product_category_level1 — top category

section.reference: Content API helper listing disapproved and warned products by SKU from Merchant Center

rule.statement: Treat the product feed as the campaign; 80% of Shopping optimization is feed quality (titles, images, categories, GTINs), not bid adjustments.
rule.rationale: Shopping has no ad copy — Google assembles the ad from the feed. A product with a vague title and missing GTIN will receive few or no impressions regardless of bid, because the query matching quality is determined by the feed, not bids.

rule.statement: Place the most search-relevant terms in the first 60 characters of the product title; mobile truncation cuts anything beyond that.
rule.rationale: Mobile Shopping results truncate titles at ~60 characters. The most important differentiating attributes (brand, model, color, size) must be front-loaded to appear before truncation.

list.item: Title pattern: [Brand] [Product Type] [Color/Size/Material] [Model] [Key Feature]
Avoid: "BEST", "SALE", all-caps, special characters, promotional copy in titles
GTIN required for branded products — materially improves impression eligibility
Google Product Category required — improves matching and reporting
High-quality images: minimum 800x800 for standard, 1200x1200 preferred
Update price and availability at least daily; stale data triggers Merchant Center suspension

rule.statement: Structure Shopping campaigns by product margin tier or category, not arbitrarily; bidding strategy needs clusters with similar ROAS targets.
rule.rationale: A single Shopping campaign mixing high-margin and low-margin products cannot optimize bids for both simultaneously. Separate campaigns or ad groups with different bids per margin tier produce materially better ROAS.

rule.statement: Use campaign priority settings when running multiple Shopping campaigns over the same SKUs; without priority settings, Google chooses the campaign randomly and budget allocation becomes unpredictable.
rule.rationale: Priority (Low/Medium/High) determines which campaign wins the internal auction when multiple campaigns target the same product. Using it enables a branded-vs-non-branded or promotional-vs-standard split that is otherwise uncontrollable.

list.item: Start: Manual CPC — full per-product-group bid control
After 30+ conversions per campaign: switch to Target ROAS
Adjust bids by product performance: high-ROAS groups up, low-ROAS groups down
Bid adjustments available: device, location

rule.statement: Do not pause and unpause Shopping campaigns frequently; each pause disrupts auction history and Smart Bidding learning.
rule.rationale: Shopping's Smart Bidding model is built on conversion history per product. Frequent pausing erases the optimizer's signal and restarts the learning phase, producing inefficient bids for 1-2 weeks after each restart.

rule.statement: Maintain two separate service-account credential sets: Content API scope for Merchant Center operations and Google Ads API scope for campaign operations; never conflate customer_id (Ads) with merchant_id (GMC).
rule.rationale: The two APIs have different authentication scopes, rate quotas, and ID namespaces. An agent that mixes them will authenticate against the wrong service and produce confusing 401 or resource-not-found errors.

rule.statement: API quotas are separate: Content API has its own quota distinct from Google Ads API quota; bulk feed uploads can starve campaign-management calls if not rate-limited independently.
rule.rationale: A large feed upload run simultaneously with campaign setup can exhaust the Content API quota for the day, preventing further product insertions or updates until the next day's quota resets.

rule.statement: Construct full product IDs with the format "online:en:US:SKU123" (channel:language:country:item_id); using just the SKU without the prefix will fail with resource-not-found errors.
rule.rationale: Content API product IDs include channel (online/local), locale, target country, and the store-defined item ID. The bare SKU alone is not a valid Content API resource identifier.

rule.statement: Merchant Center price.value is a decimal string like "29.99" with a separate price.currency field; it is NOT micros. Do not apply Google Ads micros logic (multiply by 1,000,000) to Content API price fields.
rule.rationale: Agents working across Google Ads and Content API in the same session commonly apply the micros convention to Content API and submit prices as "29990000" — which lists products at $29 million and triggers immediate disapproval.

rule.statement: Run the Merchant Center diagnostics daily; a single critical policy issue suspends the entire account, taking down both Shopping and Performance Max simultaneously.
rule.rationale: GMC suspensions are account-wide, not product-level. One misrepresentation or policy violation immediately stops all Shopping ad serving for every campaign linked to that account until the appeal is approved (1-3 weeks).

antipattern.description: Treating feed upload as synchronous — checking product status immediately after upload.
antipattern.reason: Feed upload is async; productstatuses.get reflects the state before the upload for up to 30 minutes after insertion. Agents checking status immediately after upload will see stale disapprovals.

list.item: Title rewrites for low-impression products: human brand review required
Policy disapproval appeals: human legal review required
Target ROAS changes above 20%: human approval required
New country expansion (new feed + shipping settings): human approval required

template.purpose: Content API helper listing disapproved and warned products by SKU from Merchant Center
